title: The New Covenant: Hebrews 8:8-13
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The New Covenant_ Hebrews 8_8-13 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Hebrews 8:8-12 quoting Jeremiah 31:31-34 — the longest OT quote in the NT
I will put My laws in their minds and write them on their hearts (v.10)
All shall know Me from the least to the greatest (v.11)
I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sins no more (v.12)
By saying ‘a new covenant’ He has made the first obsolete (v.13)
summary: Continuation of the Hebrews 8 sermon, focusing on vv.8-13 and the extended quotation of Jeremiah 31:31-34—the longest Old Testament quotation in the New Testament. The New Covenant promises inward transformation (the law written on hearts), universal knowledge of God among His people, and complete and final forgiveness. The author’s conclusion: by proclaiming a new covenant, God declared the first obsolete.
transcript:
## The New Covenant: Hebrews 8:7–13

God in his great mercy speaks to us in his word in a way we can understand — by way of covenant. John Calvin said that God condescends to us in Scripture, speaking to us in what he called "baby talk." The God of the universe stoops to speak to us in terms we can grasp.

Two weeks ago we began considering the new covenant from Hebrews 8, because the theme of the new covenant is so central to 2 Corinthians 3–4, which we have been following in our morning service. Today we finish this passage.

This is the longest single, continuous quotation of the Old Testament in the New Testament — Jeremiah 31:31–34, reproduced at length. The new covenant is replacing — or better, *fulfilling* — the old covenant, also called the Mosaic covenant: the covenant made at Sinai and in the wilderness.

The whole book of Hebrews makes this argument: in Christ we have a better mediator, a better high priest, a better tabernacle, a better sacrifice. Christ is better than the angels, better than Moses, better than Aaron. And as Paul writes in Romans 8:3–4:

> *"For what the law could not do in that it was weak through the flesh, God did by sending His own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, on account of sin: He condemned sin in the flesh, that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us who do not walk according to the flesh but according to the Spirit."*

## Question 1: When Was the New Covenant Inaugurated?

The new covenant was not inaugurated by a single event but by the entirety of Christ's life, death, resurrection, ascension, and session — his being seated at the right hand of the Father, which means both that he reigns and that his work is complete.

If we choose one event as the formal inauguration, it is the institution of the Lord's Supper, where Jesus himself declares:

> *"This cup is the new covenant in My blood."* (Luke 22:20)

Some point to the future tenses in Hebrews 8 — "I will make," "I will put" — as evidence that the new covenant awaits a future millennial fulfillment. But the author of Hebrews is quoting from Jeremiah, where those were future tenses *from Jeremiah's perspective*. The author quotes the passage precisely to say: what was once future to Jeremiah is present reality now, because of Jesus. The future tenses belong to the quotation, not to the author's own viewpoint.

That said, the new covenant is a *progressive* covenant. Aspects of its promises are already fulfilled; others await the return of Christ for their complete fulfillment.

## Question 2: With Whom Was This Covenant Made?

The text is explicit:

> *"Behold, the days are coming, says the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah."* (Hebrews 8:8)

God makes the new covenant with the house of Israel and Judah. This raises an obvious question: if this covenant was made with Israel, what does it have to do with Gentile believers?

The answer is that if you are trusting in Jesus Christ and have been baptized, you *are* a member of Israel — the Israel of God. The church is not a replacement for Israel but a *continuation* of Israel. Paul writes:

> *"If some of the branches were broken off, and you, being a wild olive tree, were grafted in among them, and with them became a partaker of the root and fatness of the olive tree..."* (Romans 11:17)

There is one olive tree — the people of God — and Gentile believers have been grafted into it. The tree has not changed. Paul says in Ephesians 2:11–13 that Gentiles who were once "without Christ, being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel," have been "brought near by the blood of Christ." And in Galatians 6:16, he calls the church "the Israel of God."

So rest assured: all the promises of the new covenant — the promises quoted here in Hebrews 8 — are yours in Jesus Christ, because you are part of the Israel of God.

## Question 3: What Are the Promises of the New Covenant?

**First promise:** If you cling to Jesus Christ, you cannot fail. God will not disregard you (Hebrews 8:9 by contrast). The security of the new covenant is grounded in Christ, not in our performance.

**Second promise:** The law of God is written on our hearts and minds (verse 10). This is the promise of the new heart from Ezekiel 36:26–27 — God removes the heart that is unable and unwilling to love and trust him and gives a heart that loves him. This was true for Old Testament believers as well, but the new covenant brings a greater and wider expression of this work.

**Third promise:** God is our God and we are his people (verse 10). This is the basic promise of every covenant in the Bible — from Genesis to Revelation. Jesus is called Emmanuel, "God with us." Revelation 21:3 declares: "God Himself will be with them and be their God."

**Fourth promise:** An intimate, personal, direct knowledge of God (verse 11). Jesus quotes this same passage in John 6 in connection with salvation. The Spirit applies the work of Christ to us personally.

**Fifth promise:** Complete forgiveness of sins (verse 12):

> *"For I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their lawless deeds I will remember no more."* (Hebrews 8:12)

In the old covenant, God "passed by" or "winked at" sins because the atonement was still to come. In the new covenant, sins are completely removed — placed on Christ at the cross. God holds the righteousness of Christ over those who are in him, not their sin.

## Question 4: What Are the Curses of the New Covenant?

> *"In that He says, 'A new covenant,' He has made the first obsolete. Now what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away."* (Hebrews 8:13)

There is only one covenant available. If you refuse this one, there is no hope left.

> *"For if we sin willfully after we have received the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a certain fearful expectation of judgment, and fiery indignation which will devour the adversaries."* (Hebrews 10:26–27)

> *"It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God."* (Hebrews 10:31)

The cup in the Lord's Supper signifies this reality. Christ drank the cup of the wrath of God — at Gethsemane he prayed, "Let this cup pass from Me," and then he drank it — so that we might drink the cup of his goodness and forgiveness. If you refuse him, that cup of wrath remains yours. If you receive him, you drink only blessing.

## Conclusion: The Freedom of the Covenant

Take this doctrine to heart and see what it produces. Because you are a participant by faith in the new covenant, you have freedom to obey Christ. You have deliverance from guilt, because all your sin has been placed on Christ. You have freedom to be honest in your relationships, to serve others, to face difficulty, and to rejoice — because nothing can separate you from Jesus Christ.

> *"What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare His own Son, but delivered Him up for us all, how shall He not with Him also freely give us all things?"* (Romans 8:31–32)

That is the new covenant. Live in it.
